title: Lazarus, Our Friend, Sleeps
subtitle: The sisters send their grief to Christ, and He waits across the Jordan

stillness: Be still, and let the world's brightness fall.
As the bride says: I sleep, and my heart keeps watch.
Quiet your soul now, and wait upon the Lord.
gospel: Now a certain man was sick, Lazarus of Bethania,
of the town of Mary and her sister Martha.
The sisters therefore sent to Him, saying:
Lord, behold, he whom Thou lovest is sick.
When Jesus heard it, He said:
This sickness is not unto death,
but for the glory of God.
And He remained two days still in the same place.
Then He said: Lazarus our friend sleepeth;
but I go that I may wake him out of sleep.
Then therefore Jesus said to them plainly:
Lazarus is dead. And I am glad, for your sake.
And they went, and came near to Bethania.
behold: Attend to each thing, as though you were present.
Behold the sisters, sorrowing, who will not leave their house;
they do not say, Come and heal; they do not dare to command.
They say only: Lord, behold, he whom Thou lovest is sick.
As if to say: It is enough that Thou knowest him.
For Thou dost not love, and then abandon.
Behold the sick man, the friend who often showed Him kindness.
The one languishing, these two grieving, all of them beloved.
Behold the wondrous goodness and care of the Lord toward His own.
consider: Mark this: those whom Christ loves more are sometimes struck down more.
As a physician, fearing a deadly fever,
brings on a lesser one, that the deadly be escaped;
so Christ sends bodily sickness,
that the perilous disease of the soul be turned aside,
for we are prone to sin and to the doing of evil.
Hugh says: Foreseeing that some can sin much,
He scourges them, that they sin not;
for it is more useful to be broken by sickness unto salvation
than to remain whole unto damnation.
He delayed to heal, that He might raise the dead.
He waited until the fourth day was complete,
that none could say it was a swoon, and not death.
Their brother's death He calls a sleeping and a slumber,
for what to others is death, to the Lord is only sleep:
for it is as easy, nay easier, for Christ to raise the dead from the tomb
than for another to wake a sleeper from his bed.
We are set in this world like an arrow before a mark;
the more often death's arrow is loosed after us,
the nearer and more perilously it strikes — unless we are corrected.
prayer: Lord Jesus, I behold Thy wondrous goodness toward Thine own.
I do not say, Come and heal; I dare not command Thee.
I say only: behold, he whom Thou lovest is sick.
It is enough that Thou knowest, for Thou dost not love and abandon.
Thou who art the consoler of the grieving, the healer of the languishing,
if Thou dost scourge me, break me unto salvation,
and let me not remain whole unto damnation.
Call my death a sleep, for the hope of resurrection;
and wake me, when Thou wilt, out of sleep.
Strengthen me in faith, that seeing, I may more firmly believe in Thee.

latinJewel.latin: Ille languens, istæ tristes, omnes dilecti.
latinJewel.english: He was the one languishing, these two were grieving, and all of them were beloved.
